title: Installation
body:
Install Grove

Add Grove to your Go module:

go get grove

Grove requires Go 1.24 or later. It has no runtime dependencies beyond the Go standard library — the only external package is testify, used exclusively for tests.

Create an Engine

The engine is the main entry point for compiling and rendering templates. Provide a store that loads template source files:

package main

import (
	"context"
	"fmt"
	grove "grove/pkg/grove"
)

func main() {
	store := grove.NewFileSystemStore("./templates")
	eng := grove.New(grove.WithStore(store))

	result, err := eng.Render(context.Background(), "hello.grov", grove.Data{
		"name": "World",
	})
	if err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
	fmt.Println(result.Body)
}

The FileSystemStore loads templates from the specified directory. The engine compiles templates to bytecode on first use and caches them in an LRU cache (default 512 entries). Compiled bytecode is immutable and safe for concurrent use.

title: Quick Start
body:
Your First Template

Create a template file templates/hello.grov:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
  <h1>Hello, {{ name }}!</h1>
  <p>Today is {{ day }}.</p>
</body>
</html>

Variables are interpolated with double curly braces {{ }}. Values are auto-escaped by default to prevent XSS.

Render from Go

Pass data as a grove.Data map (alias for map[string]any):

result, err := eng.Render(ctx, "hello.grov", grove.Data{
	"name": "Grove",
	"day":  "Monday",
})
if err != nil {
	log.Fatal(err)
}
fmt.Println(result.Body)

Using MemoryStore

For testing or embedding templates in Go code, use MemoryStore:

store := grove.NewMemoryStore()
store.Set("greet.grov", "Hello, {{ name | upper }}!")
eng := grove.New(grove.WithStore(store))
result, _ := eng.Render(ctx, "greet.grov", grove.Data{"name": "world"})
// result.Body == "Hello, WORLD!"

The RenderResult also contains collected assets, meta tags, and hoisted content from the template render pass.

title: Template Basics
body:
Variable Interpolation

Output a variable using double curly braces. Grove auto-escapes HTML by default:

{{ user.name }}
{{ product.price }}
{{ items | length }}

To output raw HTML, use the safe filter: {{ content | safe }}.

Comments

Comments use {# #} syntax and are stripped from output entirely:

{# This is a comment — it won't appear in the rendered HTML #}
{# TODO: add breadcrumbs here #}

Whitespace Control

Whitespace in templates is preserved by default. Block tags like {% if %} and {% for %} do not add extra whitespace beyond what is in the source. Template files are rendered character-for-character except for tag and expression nodes.

Delimiters

Grove uses three delimiter pairs:

{{ expr }}     {# output expression #}
{% tag %}      {# block tag (if, for, block, etc.) #}
{# comment #}  {# stripped from output #}

title: Variables and Expressions
body:
Variable Access

Variables are looked up in the current scope. Dot notation accesses object properties and map keys:

{{ user.name }}
{{ config.site.title }}
{{ items.0 }}

Arithmetic

Grove supports standard arithmetic operators inside expressions:

{{ price * quantity }}
{{ total - discount }}
{{ offset + limit }}
{{ count / pages }}
{{ index % 2 }}

Comparisons and Logic

Use comparison operators for boolean expressions. Logical operators use English keywords:

{% if user.age >= 18 and user.verified %}
  Welcome!
{% endif %}

{% if not item.available or item.stock == 0 %}
  Out of stock
{% endif %}

String Concatenation

Use ~ to concatenate strings (auto-converts non-strings):

{{ "Hello, " ~ name ~ "!" }}
{{ "/docs/" ~ section.slug ~ "/" ~ page.slug }}

Ternary Operator

Inline conditionals with ? : syntax:

{{ active ? "active" : "inactive" }}
{{ count == 1 ? "item" : "items" }}
<option {{ selected == opt ? "selected" : "" }}>{{ opt }}</option>

title: Filters
body:
Pipe Syntax

Filters transform a value using the pipe | operator:

{{ name | upper }}
{{ description | truncate(100) }}
{{ price | round(2) }}

Filter Chaining

Chain multiple filters — they apply left to right:

{{ title | lower | truncate(50) }}
{{ tags | join(", ") | upper }}
{{ body | striptags | truncate(160) }}

Filters with Arguments

Some filters accept arguments in parentheses:

{{ text | truncate(80, "…") }}
{{ name | replace("World", "Grove") }}
{{ items | batch(3) }}
{{ num | round(2) }}

Common Filters

Grove includes 40+ built-in filters. A few essentials:

{{ name | upper }}           {# GROVE #}
{{ name | lower }}           {# grove #}
{{ name | title }}           {# Grove Docs #}
{{ text | truncate(50) }}    {# truncates with ... #}
{{ items | length }}         {# count of items #}
{{ list | join(", ") }}      {# join with separator #}
{{ value | default("N/A") }} {# fallback if nil/empty #}
{{ html | safe }}            {# bypass auto-escaping #}

See the Filter Reference for the complete list.

title: Control Flow
body:
if / elif / else

Conditionals use {% if %}, {% elif %}, and {% else %}:

{% if user.admin %}
  <a href="/admin">Admin Panel</a>
{% elif user.moderator %}
  <a href="/mod">Mod Tools</a>
{% else %}
  <a href="/profile">My Profile</a>
{% endif %}

for / empty

Loop over lists with {% for %}. The optional {% empty %} block renders when the list is empty:

{% for item in items %}
  <li>{{ item.name }}</li>
{% empty %}
  <li>No items found.</li>
{% endfor %}

range()

Use range() to generate numeric sequences:

{% for i in range(1, 6) %}
  <p>Step {{ i }}</p>
{% endfor %}

{# range(stop), range(start, stop), range(start, stop, step) #}

set and let

Assign variables with {% set %}. Use {% let %}...{% endlet %} for multiple assignments:

{% set title = page.title | upper %}
{% set href = "/docs/" ~ section.slug ~ "/" ~ page.slug %}

{% let %}
  first = items | first
  count = items | length
{% endlet %}

capture

Capture rendered output into a variable:

{% capture greeting %}
  Hello, {{ name }}! You have {{ count }} messages.
{% endcapture %}

{{ greeting | upper }}

title: Template Inheritance
body:
extends and block

A child template extends a parent using {% extends %}, then overrides named {% block %} regions:

{# base.grov #}
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>{% block title %}My Site{% endblock %}</title>
</head>
<body>
  {% block content %}{% endblock %}
</body>
</html>
{# page.grov #}
{% extends "base.grov" %}

{% block title %}My Page — My Site{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
  <h1>Hello from page.grov</h1>
{% endblock %}

super()

Call {{ super() }} inside a block to include the parent block's content, then add to it:

{# docs-layout.grov #}
{% extends "base.grov" %}

{% block nav %}
  {{ super() }}
  <div class="breadcrumb-bar">...</div>
{% endblock %}

Multi-Level Inheritance

Grove supports unlimited inheritance depth. A child can extend a parent that extends a grandparent:

base.grov          {# HTML shell, nav, footer #}
  docs-layout.grov {# adds sidebar, uses super() #}
    page.grov      {# fills content block #}

Each level only overrides the blocks it cares about. Unoverridden blocks use their parent's content.

title: Includes and Partials
body:
include

The {% include %} tag inlines another template, sharing the current scope:

{% include "partials/header.grov" %}
{% include "partials/footer.grov" %}

The included template can read and write variables in the parent scope.

render

The {% render %} tag renders a template in an isolated scope. Pass data explicitly as named parameters:

{% render "partials/card.grov" title=item.title body=item.body %}
{% render "partials/sidebar.grov" sections=sections current_slug=page.slug %}

Because render uses isolated scope, the partial cannot accidentally read or modify parent variables. This makes partials more predictable and reusable.

When to Use Which

Use include for fragments that are tightly coupled to the parent template (e.g., a nav bar that reads site globals). Use render for self-contained components that should receive all their data explicitly.

title: Components
body:
component and slot

Components let you define reusable UI blocks with named slots for content injection. Define a component template with {% slot %} placeholders:

{# components/card.grov #}
{% props title, variant = "default" %}
<div class="card card-{{ variant }}">
  <div class="card-header">{{ title }}</div>
  <div class="card-body">
    {% slot body %}{% endslot %}
  </div>
  <div class="card-footer">
    {% slot footer %}<!-- empty -->{% endslot %}
  </div>
</div>

Calling Components

Use {% component %} with {% fill %} to inject content into slots:

{% component "components/card.grov" title="My Card" variant="primary" %}
  {% fill body %}
    <p>This goes into the body slot.</p>
  {% endfill %}
  {% fill footer %}
    <a href="/more">Read more</a>
  {% endfill %}
{% endcomponent %}

Props declared with {% props %} can have default values (like variant = "default"). Undeclared props are not accessible inside the component template.

title: Macros
body:
Defining Macros

Macros are reusable template functions. Define them with {% macro %}...{% endmacro %}:

{% macro input(name, value, type="text") %}
  <input type="{{ type }}" name="{{ name }}" value="{{ value }}">
{% endmacro %}

{% macro badge(label, color="green") %}
  <span class="badge badge-{{ color }}">{{ label }}</span>
{% endmacro %}

Calling Macros

Call macros inline using their name:

{{ input("email", user.email, "email") }}
{{ badge("New") }}
{{ badge("Admin", "red") }}

Importing Macros

Import a macro file with {% import %}...{% as %} to use its macros as a namespace:

{% import "macros/forms.grov" as forms %}
{% import "macros/ui.grov" as ui %}

{{ forms.input("email", user.email) }}
{{ ui.badge("New") }}

Macros have their own isolated scope — they cannot read variables from the parent template. All data must be passed as arguments.

title: Asset Management
body:
asset

The {% asset %} tag registers a CSS or JS file to be injected into the page head or footer. Assets are deduplicated and sorted by priority:

{% asset "/static/app.css" type="stylesheet" priority=10 %}
{% asset "/static/vendor.js" type="script" priority=1 %}
{% asset "/static/app.js" type="script" priority=5 %}

Assets with type="stylesheet" are collected by result.HeadHTML(). Assets with type="script" are collected by result.FootHTML(). In your handler, inject them into the HTML:

body = strings.Replace(body, "<!-- HEAD_ASSETS -->", result.HeadHTML(), 1)
body = strings.Replace(body, "<!-- FOOT_ASSETS -->", result.FootHTML(), 1)

meta

The {% meta %} tag registers meta tags collected in result.Meta:

{% meta name="description" content="My page description" %}
{% meta name="og:title" content=page.title %}

hoist

The {% hoist %} tag collects arbitrary HTML strings into named buckets via result.GetHoisted(bucket):

{% hoist "head" %}
  <link rel="canonical" href="/docs/{{ page.slug }}">
{% endhoist %}

title: Sandboxing
body:
SandboxConfig

Use WithSandbox to restrict what templates are allowed to do. This is useful when rendering user-provided templates or running an untrusted template environment:

eng := grove.New(
	grove.WithStore(store),
	grove.WithSandbox(grove.SandboxConfig{
		AllowedTags: []string{
			"if", "elif", "else", "for", "empty", "set",
		},
		AllowedFilters: []string{
			"upper", "lower", "title", "truncate", "default",
		},
		MaxLoopIter: 1000,
	}),
)

AllowedTags

When AllowedTags is non-nil, any tag not in the list causes a ParseError at compile time. Set to nil to allow all tags.

AllowedFilters

When AllowedFilters is non-nil, any filter not in the list causes a ParseError. Set to nil to allow all filters.

MaxLoopIter

MaxLoopIter limits the total number of loop iterations per render pass (across all loops in the template tree). Set to 0 for unlimited. Exceeding the limit causes a RuntimeError.

title: Go Integration
body:
GroveResolve Interface

To expose Go structs as template objects with dot-notation access, implement GroveResolve:

type Product struct {
	Name  string
	Price float64
}

func (p Product) GroveResolve(key string) (any, bool) {
	switch key {
	case "name":  return p.Name, true
	case "price": return p.Price, true
	}
	return nil, false
}

result, _ := eng.Render(ctx, "product.grov", grove.Data{
	"product": Product{Name: "Widget", Price: 9.99},
})

In templates: {{ product.name }}, {{ product.price }}

RegisterFilter

Register custom filters with RegisterFilter. The function signature must match grove.FilterFn:

eng.RegisterFilter("currency", grove.FilterFn(func(v grove.Value, args []grove.Value) (grove.Value, error) {
	f, _ := v.ToFloat64()
	return grove.StringVal(fmt.Sprintf("$%.2f", f)), nil
}))

Then use in templates: {{ price | currency }}

SetGlobal

Register values available in every render call without passing them in Data:

eng.SetGlobal("site_name", "My Site")
eng.SetGlobal("current_year", "2026")

Stores

Grove ships two stores. FileSystemStore loads from disk; MemoryStore stores templates in a map. Implement the store.Store interface for custom backends (databases, CDNs, etc.).
